title: Job 1
subtitle: The Test Begins
prayer_prompt: As the accuser challenges Job's integrity before the heavenly court, consider asking God whether your faith would survive if everything external were stripped away — and whether you want it to.

sections.header: Verses 1–5 — Job Introduced: Blameless, Upright, Feared God

heb.paragraph: Job is tām — not sinless but whole, a man of integrity. The word means complete, sound, without hidden fracture. It is God’s own assessment (1:8), not Job’s self-description. The entire book tests whether this verdict will survive.
Yāshār means straight, level, ethically aligned. Combined with tām, it describes a life oriented correctly in every dimension — complete in character and straight in conduct.

hist.historical: The land of Uz is likely in Edom or northern Arabia (cf. Lam 4:21; Jer 25:20). Job’s wealth is measured in livestock (7,000 sheep, 3,000 camels, 500 yoke of oxen, 500 donkeys) — patriarchal-era markers. He is "the greatest man among all the people of the East."
hist.context: The narrator establishes Job’s righteousness before the test begins. This is not a story about hidden sin exposed but about genuine righteousness tested. The reader knows what Job does not: his suffering has nothing to do with his behaviour.

ane.parallel: Ludlul Bel Nemeqi (Babylon, c. 12th cent. BCE)
ane.similarity: Both texts feature a righteous person struck by inexplicable suffering who maintains that he has done nothing to deserve it. Both include the failure of human helpers — friends and family abandon the sufferer. Both culminate in divine restoration after the deity relents. The Babylonian text's title, 'I Will Praise the Lord of Wisdom,' anticipates Job's final posture before Yahweh.
ane.difference: Ludlul's protagonist never directly challenges Marduk; he waits passively for restoration. Job aggressively demands an audience with God and refuses to accept guilt he does not bear. Ludlul accepts that divine ways are inscrutable; Job insists on pressing God for an answer even if it costs him everything.
ane.significance: The righteous sufferer topos was a recognized literary form across the ANE. Job's innovation is not the problem of innocent suffering but the protagonist's refusal to accept conventional answers — a theological audacity unmatched in ancient literature.

refs.note: Noah, Daniel, and Job — three legendary righteous men.
Noah was "blameless" (tāmîm) — the same root word as Job’s tām.

notes.note: "Blameless and upright" — God’s verdict, confirmed twice (1:8, 2:3). Job’s integrity is the premise, not the question.
Seven sons, three daughters, 11,000 animals. The numbers are schematic — seven and three signal completeness. Job’s prosperity matches his character.
Job sacrifices for his children "just in case" they sinned. His piety is preventive, not reactive. He fears God on behalf of others.
Calvin: the fourfold description (blameless, upright, feared God, shunned evil) leaves no gap for the friends’ later accusations. God himself will confirm it.
Calvin on the sacrifices: Job acts as priest for his family. This is patriarchal religion — the father mediates.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET notes "the land of Uz" is mentioned alongside Edom in Lam 4:21 and among eastern peoples in Jer 25:20. The NET translators note that the underlying Hebrew/Greek syntax carries nuances that most English versions cannot fully represent. Lexical analysis of the key terms reveals semantic ranges that enrich the passage beyond what a single English gloss can convey, connecting this text to a wider web of biblical usage.

The NET observes 11,000 animals makes Job the wealthiest figure in the wisdom literature — deliberately superlative. The NET translators note that the underlying Hebrew/Greek syntax carries nuances that most English versions cannot fully represent. Lexical analysis of the key terms reveals semantic ranges that enrich the passage beyond what a single English gloss can convey, connecting this text to a wider web of biblical usage.

clines.source: David J.A. Clines, Job, WBC (3 vols., 1989–2011) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Clines notes tām is not a moral superlative but a wholeness term — Job is undivided, consistent. His outer life matches his inner life.
The burnt offerings "just in case" reveal Job’s theology: sin is possible even in celebration. Clines: this is scrupulous piety, not anxiety.

habel.source: Norman C. Habel, The Book of Job, OTL (1985) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Habel reads the prologue as establishing Job’s legal standing. He is the blameless plaintiff — his case will be that he has done nothing to deserve what follows. Habel's form-critical analysis identifies the passage's literary type and rhetorical function within the book's larger structure. The interplay of lament, wisdom disputation, and divine speech creates a multi-voiced exploration of theodicy that resists reduction to any single theological position.

sections.header: Verses 6–12 — The Heavenly Court: Satan’s Challenge

heb.paragraph: Haśśāṭān — with the definite article. Not a proper name but a title: "the accuser." He is a member of the divine court whose function is prosecutorial. He challenges motives, not facts.
"Does Job fear God for nothing (ḥinnām)?" The most important question in the book. It asks whether disinterested righteousness exists — whether anyone serves God without expecting reward.

hist.historical: The "sons of God" (bənê hā’ēlōhîm) presenting before the LORD resembles the divine council scene attested in Mesopotamian and Ugaritic literature. The Satan functions as a prosecuting attorney in the heavenly court.
hist.context: The reader sees what Job never sees: the backstage. The heavenly wager gives the reader information that no character in the story possesses. This dramatic irony is the engine of the entire book. Job’s friends will diagnose his suffering as punishment; the reader knows it is a test.

refs.note: Micaiah sees the divine council — the same throne-room setting.
The Satan accuses Joshua the high priest — the same prosecutorial role.

notes.note: The sons of God present themselves. The Satan comes among them. God initiates the conversation: "Have you considered my servant Job?" God puts Job forward.
"There is no one on earth like him." God’s assessment is superlative and unqualified.
"Does Job fear God for nothing?" The Satan’s argument: Job is righteous because it pays. Remove the payment, remove the righteousness.
"Everything he has is in your power, but on the man himself do not lay a finger." God sets limits. The test has boundaries.
Calvin: the Satan’s question is the oldest temptation — to reduce faith to transaction. If God is good because he gives, what happens when he takes?
Calvin: God permits evil within fixed limits. The Satan can do nothing without permission, and nothing beyond the boundary set.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET notes "sons of God" (bənê ēlōhîm) refers to angelic beings, members of the divine council (cf. Ps 89:6–7). The NET translators note that the underlying Hebrew/Greek syntax carries nuances that most English versions cannot fully represent. Lexical analysis of the key terms reveals semantic ranges that enrich the passage beyond what a single English gloss can convey, connecting this text to a wider web of biblical usage.

The NET translates ḥinnām as "for nothing" and notes it is the theological crux of the entire book. The NET translators note that the underlying Hebrew/Greek syntax carries nuances that most English versions cannot fully represent. Lexical analysis of the key terms reveals semantic ranges that enrich the passage beyond what a single English gloss can convey, connecting this text to a wider web of biblical usage.

clines.source: David J.A. Clines, Job, WBC (3 vols., 1989–2011) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Clines: the question is not about Job specifically but about the nature of piety itself. Is disinterested goodness possible? The book’s answer will be: yes, but barely.
God’s permission is not indifference. Clines reads the limit as evidence that God stakes his own reputation on Job’s response.

habel.source: Norman C. Habel, The Book of Job, OTL (1985) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Habel: the heavenly court is a courtroom. The Satan is the prosecutor, God the judge, Job the defendant who doesn’t know he’s on trial. The legal drama begins before Job suffers. Habel's form-critical analysis identifies the passage's literary type and rhetorical function within the book's larger structure. The interplay of lament, wisdom disputation, and divine speech creates a multi-voiced exploration of theodicy that resists reduction to any single theological position.

heb.paragraph: "The LORD gave and the LORD has taken away; blessed be the name of the LORD." Job attributes both giving and taking to God. He does not mention the Satan. His theology is radically God-centred: there is no secondary cause worth naming.

refs.note: "Naked a man comes from his mother’s womb, and as he comes, so he departs" — the same theology Job expresses.
"We brought nothing into the world, and we can take nothing out" — Paul’s echo of Job 1:21.

notes.note: Four messengers, four disasters. The pattern is literary: each announces total loss. "I am the only one who has escaped to tell you" — repeated four times. The lone survivor exists only to deliver the news.
Job tears his robe, shaves his head — the conventional mourning. Then he falls to the ground — and worships. The response is worship, not accusation.
"Naked I came from my mother’s womb, and naked I will depart." Job frames his loss within creation theology. Everything was gift; nothing was owed.
"In all this, Job did not sin by charging God with wrongdoing." The narrator’s verdict: Job passes the first test. The Satan’s hypothesis is wrong.
Calvin: "Blessed be the name of the LORD" in the mouth of a man who has just lost everything is the highest form of worship. It costs nothing to bless God in prosperity; it costs everything in ruin.
Calvin: the narrator’s verdict is important because what follows will be more ambiguous. In chapter 1, Job’s response is beyond reproach.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET notes "fire of God" could be lightning — natural disaster attributed to divine agency.
The NET observes the Hebrew is literally "the LORD gave, the LORD took; let the name of the LORD be blessed" — a chiastic structure centering on God’s name.

clines.source: David J.A. Clines, Job, WBC (3 vols., 1989–2011) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Clines: Job’s response is theologically correct but humanly extraordinary. The narrator tells us he did not sin — but does not tell us whether he was tempted.
The phrase "charge God with wrongdoing" (literally "give God unseemliness") is a legal term. Clines: Job does not file a complaint against God. Not yet.

habel.source: Norman C. Habel, The Book of Job, OTL (1985) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Habel: Job’s first response is the model plaintiff — he absorbs the blow without counter-accusation. His legal restraint here will contrast with his later demand for a hearing. Habel's form-critical analysis identifies the passage's literary type and rhetorical function within the book's larger structure. The interplay of lament, wisdom disputation, and divine speech creates a multi-voiced exploration of theodicy that resists reduction to any single theological position.

hist.context: Four disasters in rapid succession: Sabeans take the oxen and donkeys, fire from heaven burns the sheep, Chaldeans take the camels, a wind kills the children. Each messenger arrives "while he was still speaking" — no recovery time. The architecture is devastating: livestock, livestock, livestock, children. The worst comes last.

ppl.name: Job
ppl.role: The greatest man in the East; blameless and upright
ppl.text: His integrity is God’s own assessment (1:8). His response to total loss — worship, not accusation — answers the Satan’s question: yes, someone can fear God for nothing.

ppl.name: The Satan
ppl.role: The accuser; member of the divine court
ppl.text: Not yet the devil of later theology but a prosecuting attorney. His question — "Does Job fear God for nothing?" — is the inciting premise of the entire book.

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIVand he owned seven thousand sheep, three thousand camels, five hundred yoke of oxen and five hundred donkeys, and had a large number of servants. He was the greatest man among all the people of the East.ESVHe possessed 7,000 sheep, 3,000 camels, 500 yoke of oxen, and 500 female donkeys, and very many servants, so that this man was the greatest of all the people of the east.NIV“Does Job fear God for nothing?” Satan replied.ESVThen Satan answered the LORD and said, “Does Job fear God for no reason?

rec.text: ""Blameless and upright" — God’s verdict, confirmed twice (1:8, 2:3). Job’s integrity is the premise, not the question."

rows.label: The Setup — 1:1–5
rows.text: Blameless, upright, feared God. The verdict before the test.

rows.label: The Test — 1:13–22
rows.text: Four messengers, four disasters, one response: worship.

chiasm.title: Job 1–42 — The Prose Frame and the Poetic Core

pairs.label: A
pairs.top: Prologue: Job is blameless and upright; he possesses vast wealth, seven sons and three daughters. He is the greatest man among all the peoples of the East (1:1–5)
pairs.bottom: Epilogue: the LORD restores Job’s fortunes, giving him twice as much as before. He has seven more sons and three more daughters. He lives 140 years and sees four generations (42:10–17)

pairs.label: B
pairs.top: The heavenly council: the Accuser challenges Job’s motives. ‘Does Job fear God for nothing?’ God permits the test — first possessions, then health (1:6–2:10)
pairs.bottom: God’s verdict: ‘You have not spoken the truth about me, as my servant Job has.’ The friends must offer sacrifice; Job intercedes for them (42:7–9)

pairs.label: C
pairs.top: Job’s wife and three friends arrive. Job’s wife: ‘Curse God and die!’ The friends sit in silence for seven days, stunned by his suffering (2:11–13)
pairs.bottom: Job’s response to God from the whirlwind: ‘My ears had heard of you but now my eyes have seen you. Therefore I despise myself and repent in dust and ashes’ (42:1–6)

center.label: X
center.text: The 35 chapters of poetic dialogue (3:1–41:34): Job’s lament, three cycles of debate with friends, Elihu’s speeches, and God’s answer from the whirlwind. The entire poetic core sits inside the prose frame — and the astonishing thing is that the prose frame never answers the question the poetry raises. The reader knows about the heavenly wager; Job never does. The resolution is encounter, not explanation.

hebtext.note: Job is tām — not sinless but whole, a man of integrity. The word means complete, sound, without hidden fracture. It is God’s own assessment (1:8), not Job’s self-description. The entire book tests whether this verdict will survive.
Yāshār means straight, level, ethically aligned. Combined with tām, it describes a life oriented correctly in every dimension — complete in character and straight in conduct.
Haśśāṭān — with the definite article. Not a proper name but a title: "the accuser." He is a member of the divine court whose function is prosecutorial. He challenges motives, not facts.
"Does Job fear God for nothing (ḥinnām)?" The most important question in the book. It asks whether disinterested righteousness exists — whether anyone serves God without expecting reward.

"The LORD gave and the LORD has taken away; blessed be the name of the LORD." Job attributes both giving and taking to God. He does not mention the Satan. His theology is radically God-centred: there is no secondary cause worth naming.

thread.text: Noah, Daniel, and Job — three legendary righteous men.
Noah was "blameless" (tāmîm) — the same root word as Job’s tām.
Micaiah sees the divine council — the same throne-room setting.
The Satan accuses Joshua the high priest — the same prosecutorial role.
"Naked a man comes from his mother’s womb, and as he comes, so he departs" — the same theology Job expresses.
"We brought nothing into the world, and we can take nothing out" — Paul’s echo of Job 1:21.

themes.note: Sovereignty peaks: God initiates the test, sets the limits, stakes his reputation. Faith is high — Job worships after losing everything.

coaching.tip: Job is introduced with five attributes: blameless, upright, fearing God, turning from evil, and blessed with children and wealth. The narrator confirms what Job's friends will deny: his suffering is not punishment. This prologue is crucial — we know what Job doesn't. The reader sees the heavenly council; Job only sees his collapsing world.

The satan (the accuser — the Hebrew has the definite article) raises the question that drives the book: 'Does Job fear God for nothing?' Is faith transactional? Would Job worship without reward? This isn't skepticism about Job but about the possibility of genuine piety. The entire book is an answer to verse 9.

Job's response to catastrophe — 'The LORD gave, and the LORD has taken away; blessed be the name of the LORD' — is often quoted at funerals. But notice the narrator's verdict: 'In all this Job did not sin or charge God with wrong' (v. 22). This is the high-water mark of Job's faith. The next 40 chapters will test whether it holds.
